name: October Surprise 1980
description: The "October Surprise" theory alleges that representatives of Ronald Reagan's 1980 presidential campaign secretly negotiated with Iranian officials to delay the release of American hostages held in Iran until after the election. The supposed motivation was to prevent President Jimmy Carter from securing their release, which would have boosted his chances of re-election. Proponents claim that these negotiations involved promises of arms sales to Iran in exchange for the delayed release. The theory originated in the early 1980s, gaining traction through various journalists and political figures. Key evidence cited includes alleged meetings between Reagan campaign officials (such as William Casey) and Iranian representatives in Europe, particularly in Paris. Some accounts also mention clandestine arms deals. Mainstream scholarly perspectives largely dismiss the October Surprise theory due to a lack of credible evidence and numerous inconsistencies in the accounts of alleged participants. Multiple investigations, including those by congressional committees, have concluded that there is no convincing evidence to support the claims. Critics point to conflicting testimonies, unsubstantiated documents, and the lack of reliable corroboration as reasons to reject the theory. The theory persists, however, fueled by distrust of government and a desire to explain Carter's defeat and the subsequent Iran-Contra affair.
subtitle: Alleged Reagan-Iran Hostage Deal

claims.claim: Reagan campaign officials met with Iranian representatives in Europe.
claims.evidence: Alleged eyewitness accounts and flight logs.
claims.criticism: Conflicting accounts, lack of reliable documentation, and denials from alleged participants.

claims.claim: A deal was made to delay the release of American hostages until after the election.
claims.evidence: Supposed testimonies from intermediaries and circumstantial evidence linking Reagan's victory to the hostage release.
claims.criticism: Lack of verifiable proof, inconsistent timelines, and the complexities of Iranian internal politics.

claims.claim: The Reagan administration promised arms sales to Iran in exchange for the delayed release.
claims.evidence: Connections drawn to the later Iran-Contra affair and alleged earlier arms shipments.
claims.criticism: The Iran-Contra affair occurred several years later and involved different circumstances. No credible evidence links it directly to a pre-election deal.

keyFigures.name: Gary Sick
keyFigures.role: Author and former National Security Council staff member who initially supported the theory but later retracted his support

keyFigures.name: Robert Parry
keyFigures.role: Investigative journalist who extensively covered the theory

keyFigures.name: Lee H. Hamilton
keyFigures.role: Chairman of the House October Surprise Task Force, which investigated and dismissed the claims

timeline.event: American hostages are taken in Iran.
Ronald Reagan wins the US presidential election.
The American hostages are released shortly after Reagan's inauguration.
The October Surprise theory begins to emerge.
Congressional investigations are conducted, ultimately dismissing the theory.

sources.author: Gary Sick
sources.work: October Surprise: America's Hostages in Iran and the Election of Ronald Reagan
sources.title: Gary Sick

sources.author: Robert Parry
sources.work: Trick or Treason: The October Surprise Mystery
sources.title: Robert Parry
